name: MERN
strengths: Single language across the entire stack (JS/TS)
Huge ecosystem and community
Fast prototyping with flexible schema (MongoDB)
Excellent real-time support via Socket.io or WebSockets
weaknesses: MongoDB not ideal for complex relational data
Callback/async complexity at scale
No built-in opinionated structure

name: T3
strengths: End-to-end type safety with tRPC
Prisma ORM with schema migrations
Next.js for SSR/SSG/ISR out of the box
Excellent DX with TypeScript throughout
weaknesses: Steep learning curve for beginners
tRPC is not REST — harder to expose public APIs
Opinionated — less flexible for non-standard architectures

name: MEAN
strengths: Angular's opinionated structure suits enterprise teams
Dependency injection and module system built-in
Strong TypeScript support in Angular
Good for large teams needing conventions
weaknesses: Angular has a steep learning curve
More verbose than React/Vue alternatives
Bundle sizes can be large

name: DJANGO_REACT
strengths: Django's batteries-included approach (auth, admin, ORM)
Python ecosystem for data science and ML integration
DRF (Django REST Framework) for robust API design
Mature and battle-tested
weaknesses: Two separate languages require context switching
Slower request throughput vs Node.js for I/O-heavy tasks
Django ORM can be limiting for complex queries

name: RAILS_HOTWIRE
strengths: Fastest time-to-market for CRUD applications
Convention over configuration reduces boilerplate
Hotwire enables reactive UIs without heavy JS framework
Active Record ORM is intuitive and powerful
weaknesses: Ruby is slower than Go/Node for CPU-heavy tasks
Scaling Rails requires careful tuning
Smaller talent pool than JavaScript

name: GO_POSTGRES
strengths: Exceptional throughput and low memory footprint
Built-in concurrency with goroutines
Static binaries — simple deployment
Strong standard library
weaknesses: Verbose error handling
No generics until Go 1.18 — ecosystem catching up
Smaller pool of frontend-capable Go developers

name: SPRING_BOOT
strengths: Industry standard for Java enterprise applications
Mature ecosystem with Spring Security, Spring Data
Excellent microservices support with Spring Cloud
Strong typing and IDE tooling in Java
weaknesses: Verbose configuration and boilerplate
Slow startup time (improving with GraalVM native image)
Higher resource usage vs Node.js or Go
Steep learning curve for non-Java developers

name: LARAVEL_VUE
strengths: Inertia.js enables SPA-like UX without separate API layer
Laravel's elegant ORM (Eloquent) and migration system
Rich ecosystem: Cashier (billing), Sanctum (auth), Horizon (queues)
Cheap shared hosting options for PHP
weaknesses: PHP has a mixed reputation in some developer communities
Performance ceiling lower than Go or Node.js
Inertia tightly couples frontend/backend

name: NEXT_SUPABASE
strengths: Supabase provides instant auth, storage, and realtime out of the box
Postgres under the hood — no vendor lock-in on the database
Next.js App Router with Server Components for performance
Generous free tier — ideal for startups and MVPs
weaknesses: Supabase can be a single point of failure if self-hosting is not planned
Row-Level Security (RLS) requires learning curve
Cold starts on serverless functions

components.backend: Next.js API routes + Supabase Edge Functions

name: FASTAPI_REACT
strengths: Async-first with Python's asyncio for high concurrency
Auto-generated OpenAPI docs (Swagger UI)
Native Pydantic validation — type-safe Python
Excellent for ML model serving alongside web APIs
weaknesses: Python GIL limits true parallelism for CPU tasks
Younger ecosystem than Django
Requires an ASGI server (Uvicorn/Gunicorn) setup
